original_registry_entry.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
original_registry_entry.exact_document_title: Leg ulcer infection: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations
original_registry_entry.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE wound, vascular, microbiology, and referral adaptation
original_registry_entry.population: Adults with a leg ulcer, including possible or established infection and reassessment after treatment.
original_registry_entry.clinical_setting: Primary care, community wound care, and hospital referral assessment.
original_registry_entry.applicability_note: Exact for documenting ulcer cause context, redness or swelling beyond the ulcer, warmth, increased pain, fever, worsening, systemic illness, disproportionate pain, comorbid risk, and reviewed microbiology. It does not diagnose infection or generate antibiotics, testing, referral, or urgency.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.3 — causes, colonisation, and infection signs
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting underlying-condition context and clinician-assessed redness or swelling beyond the ulcer, warmth, increased pain, fever, severity, complication risk, and previous antibiotic exposure.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.7–1.1.11 — reassessment and microbiology-result review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting rapid worsening, non-improvement, systemic illness, pain out of proportion, previous antibiotics, microbiology sampling already performed, and results already reviewed.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.12–1.1.13 — referral and specialist advice
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording clinician-identified serious illness, diabetes, immunosuppression, lymphangitis, spreading infection, or completed referral or specialist discussion; it does not create the referral.

title: Leg ulcer infection: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations
publisher: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE wound, vascular, microbiology, and referral adaptation
population: Adults with a leg ulcer, including possible or established infection and reassessment after treatment.
intended_setting: Primary care, community wound care, and hospital referral assessment.
